audit_items.proof_trace: The EBCDIC-path `ber_pvt_fputs` (which replaces stdlib `fputs` via `#define fputs ber_pvt_fputs`) performs `strncpy(buf, str, sizeof(buf))` at L118 without any NULL check on `str`; the only caller-side guard is `assert(data != NULL)` in `ber_error_print` (bprint.c:75), which is a no-op in release builds compiled with `-DNDEBUG`. Similarly, `ber_pvt_vsnprintf` (EBCDIC, L147) calls `strchr(ptr, '%')` where `ptr = fmt` with no NULL guard on `fmt`. The proof that no null dereference occurs therefore depends entirely on callers never passing NULL, which is not enforced at runtime in production builds.
audit_items.attack_scenario: On z/OS (HAVE_EBCDIC) in a release build, an LDAP client triggers an error path in OpenLDAP that results in a logging or print call with an un-checked (potentially NULL) string argument; `fputs(NULL, fp)` maps to `ber_pvt_fputs(NULL, fp)` where `strncpy(buf, NULL, sizeof(buf))` dereferences the null pointer, crashing the LDAP server process (denial of service).
